welcome.title: Welcome to Desescalate
welcome.text: Your personal companion for managing anxiety and finding inner calm when you need it most.
welcome.encouragement: You're just a few steps away from feeling better

understand.title: Understand Your Anxiety
understand.text: Learn to identify and understand your anxiety levels. The first step to managing it is recognizing it.
understand.encouragement: Here you'll find support and understanding

tools.title: Tools at Your Fingertips
tools.text: Access breathing exercises, meditation, and scientifically proven techniques to reduce anxiety.
tools.encouragement: You have everything you need to succeed

progress.title: Your Progress Matters
progress.text: Every session counts. Watch how you improve over time and celebrate your achievements on the path to wellbeing.

errors.photoPermissionRequired: Please allow access to your photos to change your profile picture

home.title: Anxiety Levels
home.subtitle: Tap each card to learn more

1.title: Calm
1.description: I feel calm and in control.

5.title: Crisis
5.description: Very short breathing, panic, sense of danger.

1: You're doing great. Let's take a moment to relax and be grateful.
What a beautiful inner place you have now. Let's expand it a little more.
You're calm. Let's use this space to deepen it.
You're balanced. Let's take a step toward even greater wellbeing.
Everything is in harmony. We can take this peace to a deeper level.
2: Good that you're here. A small pause will be enough to return to your center.
You're doing the right thing. In a few seconds, your body will relax.
Good decision. This slight tension releases easily when we give it attention.
You listened to yourself and came. That already brings you closer to your natural balance.
You're handling it well. Let's just ease the load a little.
3: Well done for stopping. Let's slow down the pace together.
Good that you came. This worry is manageable with a moment of calm.
You're on time. Let's organize what you feel so it becomes lighter.
You made a good decision. Your mind can find clarity little by little.
You're doing what's best for you. Let's regain focus and inner space.
4: I know this feels intense, but you're going to be okay. Let's go step by step.
Even if it doesn't seem like it now, we're going to lower this level together soon.
It's uncomfortable, I know, but this sensation will calm down. I'm with you.
You may not see it now, but your body can recover balance. Let's help you.
You don't have to solve anything right now. Let's breathe and this will get better.
5: I know it's overwhelming, but you're going to be okay. I'm with you right now.
Even if you don't feel it now, we're going to lower this level until you return to your true self.
Don't worry, this will pass. Let's recover one breath at a time.
It's very uncomfortable, I know, but soon you'll feel better. Just stay with me here.
It may seem impossible now, but your body can calm down. Let's start very slowly.

sameLevel: Even though the number didn't change, your body has already started moving toward calm. Let's continue.
This is completely normal. Sometimes progress is internal before showing outside. Let's take one more step.
Your system is already responding, it just needs a little more time. You're on the right path.
Well done. You're doing it. Let's continue with the next exercise to deepen the change.
All progress starts like this: slow but steady. You've already activated the process; let's continue so your body finishes regulating.
increased: It's okay to feel this way. Anxiety comes in waves and this is completely normal. I'm here with you.
Your body is going through a difficult moment, but that doesn't mean you've failed. Let's help you through this.
Sometimes anxiety rises before it falls. It's part of the process. Let's continue together, one step at a time.
Don't worry about the number. What matters is that you're here, taking care of yourself. That's already a big step.
This is temporary. Your body knows how to return to calm and we'll guide you. Breathe, I'm with you.
How brave you are for continuing. Anxiety doesn't define your strength. Let's keep going together.
This moment will pass. Now let's focus on what you can do: breathe and move forward with me.
improved: Excellent! You managed to lower the intensity. This is an important step toward your wellbeing.
Very good. Your body responded and you're now at a calmer level.
You did it. You're regaining control and that deserves recognition.
Congratulations! This shows you can regulate your mind and body. Let's continue with what corresponds to this new level.
Well done. This decrease shows your system is balancing.
Great job. Each level down is a real victory. Let's keep building from here.
Your effort is paying off. Now you're at a safer and more manageable level.
This improvement is no coincidence: you generated it. Let's keep moving forward from here.
You're doing very well. Let's take advantage of this new level to strengthen your calm.
Excellent progress. Your body and mind are listening to you. Let's continue with the exercises for this level.

welcome.title: Welcome to Desescalate
welcome.description: This app will help you manage your anxiety effectively. We'll guide you through the main features.

anxietyLevels.title: Anxiety Levels
anxietyLevels.description: Here you can select your current anxiety level from 1 to 5. Each level has personalized exercises and tips for you.

howItWorks.title: How a Session Works
howItWorks.description:
1. Select your anxiety level
2. Choose recommended exercises
3. Receive personalized tips
4. Track your progress

statistics.title: Statistics
statistics.description: In this tab you can see your session history, progress charts, and statistics about your anxiety management.

profile.title: Your Profile
profile.description: Here you can update your personal information, change your profile picture, and manage your account.

ready.title: Ready to Start
ready.description: You now know the main features. Start by selecting your current anxiety level and discover exercises and tips for you.

exercises.title: Exercises
exercises.subtitle: For {{levelTitle}} Anxiety (Level {{level}})
exercises.actionTitle: What would you like to do now?

cardiacCoherence.title: Cardiac Coherence 5-5
cardiacCoherence.description: Inhale for 5 seconds, exhale for 5 seconds. Repeat for 10 cycles. This technique activates heart rate variability and promotes deep relaxation.
cardiacCoherence.science: Based on HeartMath studies and resonant breathing by Benson and Dispenza.

diaphragmaticBreathing336.title: Gentle Diaphragmatic Breathing (3-3-6)
diaphragmaticBreathing336.description: Inhale 3 seconds, hold 3 seconds, exhale 6 seconds. Extended exhalation reduces amygdala activity.
diaphragmaticBreathing336.science: Based on Andrew Huberman's research on extended exhalation.

bodyScanQuick.title: Quick Body Scan
bodyScanQuick.description: Mentally scan your body from feet to head with gentle breathing. Notice sensations without judging them.
bodyScanQuick.science: Jon Kabat-Zinn technique from the MBSR (Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction) program.

microMeditationAppreciation.title: Micro-meditation of Appreciation
microMeditationAppreciation.description: Think of something that went well today for 20-40 seconds. Let the feeling of gratitude expand in your body.
microMeditationAppreciation.science: Based on Benson's relaxation response and gratitude studies.

calmVisualization.title: Brief Calm Visualization
calmVisualization.description: Imagine a safe and peaceful space while breathing slowly. Visualize the details: colors, sounds, sensations.
calmVisualization.science: Joe Dispenza technique for maintaining stable physiology.

tensionRelease.title: Muscle Tension-Release (2 zones)
tensionRelease.description: Tense your shoulders for 5 seconds and release. Then tense your hands for 5 seconds and release. Feel how the tension dissolves.
tensionRelease.science: Jacobson's progressive muscle relaxation technique for sympathetic reduction.

breathing46.title: 4-6 Breathing
breathing46.description: Inhale for 4 seconds, exhale for 6 seconds. Repeat for 1-2 minutes. Extended exhalation activates the parasympathetic system.
breathing46.science: Based on Huberman's research on stress reduction.

neckStretch.title: Mindful Trapezius and Neck Stretch
neckStretch.description: Tilt your head to one side, exhale while releasing tension. Repeat on the other side. Do it with full attention.
neckStretch.science: Somatic release studies combined with mindfulness.

breathCounting.title: Counting Breaths
breathCounting.description: Count from 1 to 10 with each exhalation. Do it slowly. If you lose count, start again without judging yourself.
breathCounting.science: Jon Kabat-Zinn technique to anchor attention to the present.

sensoryGrounding2.title: Simple Sensory Grounding (2 senses)
sensoryGrounding2.description: Notice something you see and something you touch. Describe them mentally in detail. This anchors you to the present moment.
sensoryGrounding2.science: Grounding reduces sympathetic nervous system activity.

grounding54321.title: Complete Grounding 5-4-3-2-1
grounding54321.description: Identify 5 things you see, 4 you touch, 3 you hear, 2 you smell, 1 you taste. Take your time with each one.
grounding54321.science: MBSR technique combined with somatic regulators.

physiologicalSigh.title: Physiological Sigh (Double Inhale)
physiologicalSigh.description: Inhale deeply, add an additional mini-inhale, then exhale long and slow. Repeat several times.
physiologicalSigh.science: Huberman technique for immediate distress reduction.

emotionalLabeling.title: Emotional Labeling
emotionalLabeling.description: Name what you feel: 'This is worry. It's not danger.' Naming emotions reduces their intensity.
emotionalLabeling.science: UCLA studies (Lieberman) show that naming reduces amygdala activity.

feetAttention.title: Attention on Feet
feetAttention.description: Feel your feet supported on the ground. Notice the pressure, temperature, contact. Breathe slowly.
feetAttention.science: Somatic grounding technique for body reconnection.

extendedExhale2x.title: Extended Exhale 2x
extendedExhale2x.description: Inhale 3 seconds, exhale 6 seconds. The double exhale activates vagal tone and calms the nervous system.
extendedExhale2x.science: Based on vagal tone and autonomic regulation studies.

physiologicalSighRepeated.title: Repeated Physiological Sigh
physiologicalSighRepeated.description: Inhale, add a mini-inhale, exhale long. Repeat for 10 cycles. It's the fastest known mechanism to reduce acute stress.
physiologicalSighRepeated.science: Andrew Huberman technique validated for acute stress.

breathing478.title: 4-7-8 Breathing
breathing478.description: Inhale 4 seconds, hold 7 seconds, exhale 8 seconds. This technique deeply activates the parasympathetic system.
breathing478.science: Andrew Weil technique for vagal activation and anxiety reduction.

somaticHold.title: Somatic Self-Containment (hands on chest)
somaticHold.description: Place one hand on your chest and another on your abdomen. Feel the movement of your breathing. This activates self-regulation.
somaticHold.science: Somatic self-soothing technique for parasympathetic regulation.

tactileGrounding.title: Tactile Grounding
tactileGrounding.description: Touch a firm surface. Feel its temperature and texture. Describe it mentally. This reduces dissociation and distress.
tactileGrounding.science: Tactile grounding to reduce dissociation and distress.

countdown54321Breath.title: 5-4-3-2-1 Countdown with Long Breaths
countdown54321Breath.description: With each number, inhale 3 seconds and exhale 6. This interrupts the cognitive loop and activates the prefrontal cortex.
countdown54321Breath.science: Cognitive loop interruption and prefrontal cortex activation.

triangularBreathing.title: Triangular Breathing 3-3-3
triangularBreathing.description: Inhale 3 seconds, hold 3 seconds, exhale 3 seconds. Technique used by Navy SEALs for control under extreme stress.
triangularBreathing.science: Navy SEALs technique for nervous system control under extreme stress.

boxBreathing4444.title: Box Breathing 4-4-4-4
boxBreathing4444.description: Inhale 4, hold 4, exhale 4, hold 4. Use when the crisis subsides a bit. Regulates heart rate.
boxBreathing4444.science: Vagal stimulation for heart rate regulation.

physicalGrounding3Points.title: Physical Grounding (3 contact points)
physicalGrounding3Points.description: Find three contact points: feet on the floor, back on the chair, hands on your legs. Feel each one.

verbalAnchoring.title: Verbal Anchoring
verbalAnchoring.description: Repeat internally: 'I am here. This will pass.' Orient your mind to the present time and space.
verbalAnchoring.science: Brief crisis intervention for temporal and spatial orientation.

doubleExtendedExhale.title: Double Extended Exhale
doubleExtendedExhale.description: Exhale gently, exhale a little more, inhale normally. Use when hyperventilation is intense. Relieves chest tension.
doubleExtendedExhale.science: Carbon dioxide reduction and immediate chest relief.

tips.title: Tip
tips.subtitle: For {{levelTitle}} Anxiety (Level {{level}})
tips.actionTitle: What would you like to do now?

celebration.title: Great job!
celebration.message: You completed a full session. Remember that every step counts on your path to wellbeing.

maintainRoutine.title: Maintain Your Routine
maintainRoutine.content: A consistent routine helps keep anxiety levels low.
steps: Set regular sleep and wake times
Plan your meals at consistent times
Include time for activities you enjoy
Maintain a balance between work and rest

dailyMindfulness.title: Practice Daily Mindfulness
dailyMindfulness.content: Regular mindfulness practice strengthens your ability to manage stress.
steps: Dedicate 5-10 minutes daily to meditation
Practice mindful eating at least one meal
Take brief breathing breaks throughout the day
Observe your thoughts without judging them
Practice gratitude before bed

stayConnected.title: Stay Connected
stayConnected.content: Social connections are fundamental for emotional wellbeing.
steps: Schedule regular time with friends or family
Participate in community activities
Share your feelings with trusted people
Offer help to others when you can

identifyTriggers.title: Identify Your Triggers
identifyTriggers.content: Knowing what triggers your anxiety is the first step to managing it.
steps: Keep a journal of anxious moments
Note what you were doing when they arose
Identify patterns in your triggers
Develop specific strategies for each one

limitCaffeine.title: Limit Caffeine Intake
steps: Gradually reduce coffee consumption
Avoid caffeine after noon
Try alternatives like herbal tea
Stay hydrated with water

takeBreaks.title: Take Regular Breaks
steps: Use the Pomodoro technique (25 min work, 5 break)
Stand up and stretch every hour
Get fresh air when possible
Disconnect from screens during breaks

getMoving.title: Get Moving
steps: Walk at least 20 minutes daily
Try yoga or gentle stretching
Dance or do exercise you enjoy
Take stairs instead of elevator

challengeNegativeThoughts.title: Challenge Negative Thoughts
challengeNegativeThoughts.content: Learn to question and reframe anxious thoughts.
steps: Identify the negative thought
Ask yourself: Is this really true?
Look for evidence for and against
Create a more balanced thought

createCalmEnvironment.title: Create a Calm Environment
createCalmEnvironment.content: Your physical environment affects your mental state.
steps: Reduce clutter in your space
Use soft lighting when possible
Incorporate plants or natural elements
Create a special corner for relaxation
Use relaxing scents like lavender

practiceSelfCompassion.title: Practice Self-Compassion
practiceSelfCompassion.content: Be kind to yourself during difficult moments.
steps: Talk to yourself as you would to a friend
Recognize that everyone has difficult moments
Don't judge yourself for feeling anxious
Celebrate your small achievements

limitScreenTime.title: Limit Screen Time
limitScreenTime.content: Excess screen time can increase anxiety.
steps: Set phone-free times
Avoid screens 1 hour before bed
Turn off non-essential notifications
Do periodic digital detox

useGroundingTechniques.title: Use Grounding Techniques
useGroundingTechniques.content: Grounding helps you return to the present moment.
steps: Practice the 5-4-3-2-1 technique with your senses
Hold an object and describe its texture
Put your feet firmly on the ground
Name things around you out loud

seekSupport.title: Seek Support
seekSupport.content: You don't have to face anxiety alone.
steps: Talk to someone you trust about how you feel
Consider joining a support group
Don't be afraid to ask for help
Accept support when offered

avoidAvoidance.title: Avoid Avoidance
avoidAvoidance.content: Avoiding situations can worsen anxiety long-term.
steps: Identify what situations you're avoiding
Gradually approach what you fear
Celebrate each small step
Remember that discomfort is temporary

writeItDown.title: Write It Down
writeItDown.content: Writing can help process intense emotions.
steps: Write your worries without filtering
Describe your physical sensations
Note possible solutions
Review what you wrote with perspective

breathingChangesChemistry.title: Breathing Changes Your Chemistry
breathingChangesChemistry.content: Exhaling longer than inhaling reduces stress signals to the brain. It's the fastest tool you have.
steps: Inhale for 3 seconds
Exhale for 6 seconds
Repeat this pattern for 1-2 minutes
Notice how your body gradually relaxes

focusOnSafety.title: Focus on Safety
focusOnSafety.content: In panic moments, your safety comes first.
steps: Go to a place where you feel safe
If possible, sit or lie down
Remember that panic will pass
Repeat: This is temporary, I am safe

useYourSenses.title: Use Your Senses
useYourSenses.content: Strong sensory stimuli can interrupt panic.
steps: Hold ice in your hands
Smell something strong like mint or citrus
Listen to relaxing music or sounds
Apply cold water to your face

haveEmergencyContact.title: Have an Emergency Contact
haveEmergencyContact.content: Having someone to call can be crucial in difficult moments.
steps: Identify 1-3 trusted people
Inform them they can be your support
Have their numbers easily accessible
Practice what to say when you call them

createCrisisPlan.title: Create a Crisis Plan
createCrisisPlan.content: A prepared plan gives you structure when you need it most.
steps: Write down steps to follow during a crisis
Include techniques that have worked for you
Have emergency numbers at hand
Review and update the plan regularly

youAreNotBroken.title: You're Not Broken, You're Regulating
youAreNotBroken.content: Stress is not a sign of weakness but of activation. Each time you return to calm, you strengthen a natural ability.
steps: Recognize that your body is responding normally
This activation is temporary and will pass
You have overcome difficult moments before
Each breath brings you closer to calm

rememberThisWillPass.title: Remember: This Will Pass
rememberThisWillPass.content: Even the most difficult moments are temporary.
steps: Panic attacks usually last 10-20 minutes
You have survived difficult moments before
Your body cannot maintain panic indefinitely
After the storm comes calm

noData.title: No data yet
noData.message: Complete your first session to see your statistics here

infoBox.title: About your data
infoBox.text: These statistics show your progress through sessions. Continue using the app to get better insights into your anxiety patterns.

logOut.title: Log out
logOut.message: Are you sure you want to log out?

resetDatabase.title: Reset database
resetDatabase.message: This will delete all data and you will have to register again. Are you sure?

generateTestData.title: Generate test data
generateTestData.message: This will create historical sessions from the last year, month, and week to test the charts. Continue?

resetTutorial.title: Reset tutorial
resetTutorial.message: This will reset the interactive tutorial and take you to the Home screen.

success.databaseReset: Database reset. You can register again.
success.testDataGenerated: Historical data generated successfully. Go to the statistics tab to see it.

errors.photoPermission: Please allow access to your photos to change your profile picture

lessons.title: Lesson of the day
lessons.subtitle: Learn about your body and mind

1.title: Your nervous system is your internal regulator
1.content: Your body has two main modes: one to activate you and one to relax you. You switch between them every day without realizing it. When you understand this, you know that what you feel is not dangerous, it's just a natural change in your body.

2.title: The sympathetic system is not dangerous
2.content: When you get stressed, your body enters 'alert mode': you breathe faster and your heart beats hard. It doesn't mean something bad is happening. It's just your body turning on energy to help you. Then you can return to calm.

3.title: The parasympathetic system is your natural brake
3.content: Your body also has a 'calm mode' that activates when you exhale slowly, relax, or focus on something calm. This system slows down your mind and breathing. You can activate it whenever you want.

4.title: Adrenaline rises before your thoughts
4.content: When something stresses you, your body releases adrenaline very quickly. That makes you feel energy, heat, or nerves. It's not harm; it's a temporary reaction. By breathing slower, adrenaline drops on its own in a few minutes.

5.title: Cortisol helps you focus
5.content: Cortisol is a hormone that helps you think clearly and solve things. When you're stressed for a long time, it can rise too much. But with rest, breathing, and breaks, it returns to normal levels.

6.title: Stress has a cycle and always goes down
6.content: Stress doesn't stay the same forever. It rises, reaches a peak, and then begins to fall. The exercises you do here just help that descent be faster, smoother, and less uncomfortable.

7.title: The amygdala only detects emotions, it doesn't think
7.content: The amygdala is a part of the brain that reacts quickly when it feels something intense. It doesn't analyze or decide, it just alerts. You still have control to calm yourself, especially when you breathe slowly or focus on something real and present.

8.title: Rumination doesn't mean you're broken
8.content: When your mind repeats the same thoughts over and over, it's not because you're failing. It's your brain trying to solve something without rest. Regulating your breathing helps those thoughts decrease in intensity.

9.title: Distress is a body reaction
9.content: Sensations like tight chest, knot in stomach, or short breathing are normal physical responses to stress. Although they feel strong, they don't mean something will happen to you. Your body can return to calm.

10.title: Your body is made to return to calm
10.content: Even when you feel very activated, your system is designed to regulate itself. Nothing stays the same forever. When you do breathing or grounding exercises, you help your body return faster to its natural state of balance.

11.title: Breathing changes your chemistry
11.content: Exhaling longer than inhaling reduces stress signals to the brain. It's the fastest tool you have to change how you feel.

12.title: The double inhale lowers distress
12.content: Inhale, add a mini-inhale, exhale long. This reduces CO2 and decreases discomfort in less than a minute.

13.title: The diaphragm has emotional memory
13.content: When it tenses, your breathing shortens; when it relaxes, your mind clears. Regulating the body regulates thoughts.

14.title: Slow breathing improves decisions
14.content: More oxygen + less adrenaline = more mental clarity. You don't need to think better; you need to breathe better first.

15.title: Stress isn't fought by thinking
15.content: It's regulated first by the body, then by the mind. Breathing well changes how you feel and how you interpret things.

16.title: Calm is trainable
16.content: Every time you practice conscious breathing, you reinforce neural connections that allow you to recover faster next time.

17.title: The stressed mind predicts danger
17.content: It's not telling the truth, it's just anticipating. Alternative thought: 'This is my body activated, not real danger.'

18.title: Worry is not preparation
18.content: Worry wears you down, it doesn't solve. Alternative thought: 'I can deal with this when my body is calmer.'

19.title: Emotional urgency is deceptive
19.content: Your body tells you 'do something now', but it's almost never necessary to act. 'I can wait a few minutes before deciding.'

20.title: Catastrophic thinking is normal under stress
20.content: But it's not a prediction, it's a reflex. 'I don't need to believe everything I think when I'm activated.'

21.title: Self-criticism increases stress
21.content: Your nervous system listens to your internal tone. Alternative thought: 'I did the best I could in this moment.'

22.title: You are not your emotions
22.content: They are temporary states. 'This is a sensation, not a permanent reality.'

23.title: Anxiety exaggerates risks
23.content: It's part of human design. 'My mind is exaggerating because my body is activated.'

24.title: Feeling fear doesn't mean you're failing
24.content: It means your system is trying to help you. 'This is discomfort, not incapacity.'

25.title: Calm doesn't require solving anything
25.content: Just reducing activation. 'I can return to my center without solving everything now.'

26.title: Calming yourself strengthens your brain
26.content: Repeating exercises strengthens the prefrontal cortex, responsible for emotional self-control.

27.title: Your body learns faster than your mind
27.content: You can feel better even if you still have racing thoughts. First change the physiology, then change the interpretation.

28.title: Symptoms decrease in layers
28.content: Sometimes breathing drops first, then chest, then thoughts. Gradual progress is normal.

29.title: Regulation improves with practice
29.content: Each session here trains your nervous system to recover faster in the future, even without the app.

30.title: You're not broken; you're regulating
30.content: Stress is not a sign of weakness but of activation. Every time you return to calm, you're strengthening a natural ability.

donation.subtitle: Your donation helps us continue developing tools to help manage anxiety
donation.thankYouMessage: Every contribution, no matter the size, helps us improve the app and reach more people who need support.

success.title: Thank you!
